title: Why some memories have no narrative, only sensation
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your body remembers what your mind forgot 

 I was sitting in my therapist's office, trying to explain a feeling I couldn't name. It wasn't a memory, not...
htmlContent:
Your body remembers what your mind forgot

I was sitting in my therapist's office, trying to explain a feeling I couldn't name. It wasn't a memory, not really. There was no story attached to it. No images, no dialogue, no sequence of events. Just this dense, hot pressure in my chest and a sudden urge to disappear. I could feel my shoulders climbing toward my ears. My breath got shallow. My hands went cold.

She asked me what was happening. I said, "I don't know. I just feel... bad."

That was it. That was all I had. A full body experience with zero narrative attached. And for years, I thought this meant I was broken, that something was wrong with my memory, that I just wasn't "processing" things the right way.

Here's the thing. I was wrong. And so is most of what we've been told about memory and trauma.

We live in a culture that worships the story. We want the linear timeline, the clear beginning and middle and end. We want the protagonist who can narrate their pain into a tidy arc of growth. We want the redemption, the lesson learned, the meaning made. But what happens when your trauma doesn't come with a script? What happens when all you have is a body that crumples in certain rooms, a throat that closes around certain words, a panic that descends without warning or reason?

You start to think you're making it up. You start to think you're just broken.

You're not. Stay with me here.

Two kinds of memory, one nervous system

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain and body. His research showed something that should be obvious but somehow isn't: the brain stores memory in more than one way. There's explicit memory, the kind you can narrate. "I went to the store, bought milk, came home." That's explicit. It has a timeline, a location, a sequence. It lives in the hippocampus, a seahorse-shaped structure in your brain that tags experiences with time and place.

Then there's implicit memory. This is the memory your body holds. It has no date stamp. No location. No narrative. It's the feeling of dread you get when someone raises their voice, even if you don't know why. It's the nausea that hits you in a crowded elevator. It's the way your jaw locks up when someone touches your shoulder from behind. Implicit memory is stored in the amygdala, the brain's smoke detector, and in the body itself, in the fascia, the muscles, the nervous system.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, calls this "procedural memory." It's the memory of how to do things, not the memory of what happened. You don't remember learning to ride a bike through a story. Your body just knows how. Trauma works the same way. Your body learns the pattern, the posture, the protective response. It doesn't need a story to do this. It just needs one exposure to threat, and it locks the response in for life.

Think about it. If you touch a hot stove, you don't need to remember the exact date and time and weather conditions of that event to know not to do it again. Your hand pulls back before your conscious mind even registers the pain. That's implicit memory in action. It's fast, automatic, and completely wordless.

Trauma is no different. It's just that the "stove" was a person. Or a situation. Or a series of events that happened before you had the language to describe them.

The preverbal wound

Here's where it gets really uncomfortable. A lot of what we call "trauma without narrative" isn't just implicit memory. It's preverbal memory. This means the experience happened before you had words to describe it.

Infants and toddlers don't have fully developed hippocampi. They can't form explicit, narrative memories of what happens to them. But their bodies are recording everything. The stress hormones. The heart rate. The temperature of the room. The smell of the person holding them. The tone of voice. The tension in the arms that pick them up or put them down.

Janina Fisher, a trauma expert who trained with Bessel van der Kolk, talks about this extensively. She says that for many people, the trauma they carry is the trauma of being a baby who was left to cry too long, or handled roughly, or not held enough, or exposed to a mother's chronic stress in utero. These experiences don't become stories. They become sensations. They become a template for how the nervous system expects the world to treat you.

Wild, right? You might be walking around with a nervous system that's bracing for abandonment, and you have no idea why. You just know that certain relationships make you feel like you're dying. You just know that being alone at night triggers a wave of terror you can't explain. You just know that your body goes rigid when someone tries to hug you.

There's no story here. There's just sensation. And that's not a failure of memory. That's how memory works when the trauma happens before the language centers of the brain are online.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's book explores how trauma can be passed down through epigenetics, shaping your nervous system before you're even born.

The wellness industry lied to you about "processing"

I need to be direct here. The wellness industry has done a lot of damage by insisting that healing requires a story. Talk therapy, for all its value, has often reinforced the idea that you need to find the words, that you need to construct a narrative, that if you just talk about it enough, you'll feel better.

But here's the problem. Talking about trauma activates the same neural networks that stored the trauma in the first place. If all you do is talk, you risk retraumatizing yourself. You're just reliving the event without ever changing the body's response to it.

Peter Levine's entire career has been about challenging this assumption. He showed that animals in the wild don't get traumatized because they complete the physical response to threat. They shake. They run. They tremble. They discharge the energy. Then they go back to grazing. Humans, with our big brains and our social conditioning, override these responses. We freeze. We comply. We stay still. And the energy gets locked in the body.

That locked energy is what you feel when you have a memory with no narrative. It's not a story. It's a survival response that never got to finish. Your body is still trying to run, fight, or freeze, even though the threat is long gone.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine's work is foundational for understanding why your body holds trauma and how to release it without needing a story.

So what do you do when you have a sensation with no narrative? You don't try to force a story. You don't sit there and ask, "What happened to me?" over and over. You stay with the sensation. You let it move. You let it shake. You let it cry without knowing why. You let your body complete the response it never got to finish.

This is terrifying for most of us. We want control. We want understanding. We want to know the "why" before we feel the "what." But the body doesn't work that way. The body wants completion, not comprehension.

Learning to trust the body's language

I've spent years trying to narrate my way out of pain. I thought if I could just find the right story, the right explanation, the right insight, my body would finally calm down. It doesn't work that way. The insight comes after the release, not before. You have to let the body do its thing first.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is well-absorbed and known for its calming effects on the nervous system. It's not a cure, but it can help take the edge off while you're doing the deeper work.

Honestly, the hardest part of this whole process is learning to trust your body when it doesn't make sense. Your body will shake for no reason. Your body will cry for no reason. Your body will feel rage toward someone you love, or terror in a safe room, or grief for a loss you can't name. And your mind will try to narrate it. Your mind will say, "This must mean something. This must be about that thing that happened in 1997." But it might not be. It might just be a wave of energy that needs to move through.

Gabor Mate, the physician who wrote about the connection between stress and illness, says that trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. That's a crucial distinction. The trauma is the wound, not the event. And wounds don't have stories. They have sensations. They have heat and cold and pressure and tightness and emptiness.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate's work shows how suppressed emotion and unprocessed trauma literally shape our biology.

I don't have a neat ending for this. I wish I did. I wish I could tell you that if you just do the right somatic exercise or take the right supplement or read the right book, all the wordless memories will suddenly make sense. But they won't. That's not the point.

The point is to stop demanding that your trauma become a story. The point is to let it be what it is: a body that's been through something and hasn't finished processing it yet. The point is to sit with the sensation without needing to name it, without needing to explain it, without needing to justify it to anyone, including yourself.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Maybe nothing. Maybe a shift. Maybe a tightening. That's your implicit memory speaking. You don't have to translate it. You just have to listen.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's exploration of how stress and suppressed emotion connect to illness.
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn's work on inherited family trauma and its effects on the nervous system.
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic on Somatic Experiencing and trauma recovery.
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A well-absorbed magnesium supplement for nervous system support.
